coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots derived from organo-templated zeolites for femtosecond mode-locked fiber laser
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Zhuomin
Cao, Chunyu
Yao, Zhangming
Fan, Baolin
Zhang, Zhanpeng
Shao, Zhiping
Chu, Shaowen
Sun, Lang
Sun, Dalin
Xu, Xintong
Ruan, Shuangchen
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) have been widely investigated due to their physicochemical and optical properties. However, the nonlinear saturable absorption property and its application in laser technology is rarely reported. Here, we report the CNDs as an ideal saturable absorber (SA) for ultra-stable femtosecond mode-locked fiber laser at a wavelength of 1.5 μm. The mono-sized CNDs of 2 nm are fabricated from a dipropylamine-templated zeolite precursor calcination with a temperature of 380 °C. The nonlinear saturable absorption property of the as-synthesized CNDs is investigated by using a balanced twin-detector measurement system. The modulation depth and saturable intensity are measured to be 33 % and 3.1 MW/cm2, respectively. By inputting the CNDs SA into the erbium-doped fiber laser (EDFL) ring cavity, a highly stable mode-locked EDFL with pulse width of 388 fs and single-to-noise ratio (SNR) of 84.4 dB can be obtained. In addition, stable soliton pairs with single-pulse width of 422 fs and SNR of 84.1 dB can also be achieved. These findings demonstrate that the carbon dots can be used as an ideal SA and has promising applications in ultrafast photonics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Zeolite
Saturable absorber
Mode-locked fiber laser

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic diagram of the EDFL.
(a) Schematic of the fabrication process of CNDs. (b) Experimental (upper) and simulated (lower) XRD patterns of GIS zeotype structure. (c) The SEM image of ZnAlPO-43 crystals.
(a) The TEM (Inset: high-resolution TEM) image of the isolated CNDs. (b) The powder XRD pattern of CNDs. (c) The Raman spectrum of CNDs. (d) The Carbon K-edge EELS spectrum of CNDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of CNDs. High-resolution C 1s (b), N 1s (c), and O 1s (d) XPS of CNDs.
(a) Device of saturable absorption experiment. (b) The nonlinear absorption data of CNDs and its corresponding fitting curve.
Output characteristics of the EDFL operated in mode-locked state. (a) Pulse trains. (b) Output optical spectrum. (c) Autocorrelation trace. (d) RF spectrum.
(a) The evolution of the output spectra in 6 h. (b) The trend of the output power with pump power.
Output characteristics of the EDFL operated in bound state. (a) Pulse trains. (b) Output optical spectrum. (c) Autocorrelation trace. (d) RF spectrum.
Summary of mode-locked EDFLs based on different SA types.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots derived from organo-templated zeolites for femtosecond mode-locked fiber laser

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Integrated Circuits and Optoelectronic Chips, Shenzhen Technology University, Shenzhen 518118, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Integrated Circuits and Optoelectronic Chips

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) have been widely investigated due to their physicochemical and optical properties. However, the nonlinear saturable absorption property and its application in laser technology is rarely reported. Here, we report the CNDs as an ideal saturable absorber (SA) for ultra-stable femtosecond mode-locked fiber laser at a wavelength of 1.5 μm. The mono-sized CNDs of 2 nm are fabricated from a dipropylamine-templated zeolite precursor calcination with a temperature of 380 °C. The nonlinear saturable absorption property of the as-synthesized CNDs is investigated by using a balanced twin-detector measurement system. The modulation depth and saturable intensity are measured to be 33 % and 3.1 MW/cm2, respectively. By inputting the CNDs SA into the erbium-doped fiber laser (EDFL) ring cavity, a highly stable mode-locked EDFL with pulse width of 388 fs and single-to-noise ratio (SNR) of 84.4 dB can be obtained. In addition, stable soliton pairs with single-pulse width of 422 fs and SNR of 84.1 dB can also be achieved. These findings demonstrate that the carbon dots can be used as an ideal SA and has promising applications in ultrafast photonics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultrafast fiber lasers are widely used in spectroscopy, biomedicine, optical communication, and advanced material processing for their short pulse duration and high peak power intensity [1–5]. One of the most successful methods to obtain ultrafast fiber lasers is passively mode-lockers based on saturable absorber (SA). To date, the commercialized SA is semiconductor saturable absorber mirror (SESAM) due to their stable operating performance. However, SESAM requires sophisticated fabrication process and works only at certain wavelength. Therefore, many efforts have been devoted to find novel SA nanomaterials with excellent nonlinear optical performance for the generation of ultrafast fiber laser [6–10]. Thanks to the ultrafast carrier dynamics, tunable modulation depth, and large third-order optical nonlinearity, many low-dimensional materials SA have been developed and applied for the ultrafast laser generation [11–16]. However, the low-dimensional materials SA is not as stable as commercial SESAM, thus highly limiting their practical applications in photonics device. It is therefore of highly urgent to explore new nanomaterials with excellent nonlinear optical properties for stable pulsed fiber laser generation.

Carbon nanodots (CNDs), known as zero-dimensional nanomaterials, have been widely used in different areas, including energy storage[17], catalysis[18], bio-imaging[19], sensor[20], drug delivery[21] and laser imaging[22]. To date, various approaches have been developed for synthesizing CNDs, including microwave irradiation[23], arc discharge, laser ablation[24], electrochemical oxidation[25] and hydro/solvothermal carbonization[26,27]. Additionally, crystalline framework materials with ordered channels (zeolite or metal–organic frameworks) can provide specific nano-confined space for the efficient synthesizing of CNDs with relatively identical size[28–30]. Moreover, recent evidences show that the mono-sized CNDs exhibit specific optical properties[31–33], including multiple luminescence, room temperature phosphorescence and thermally activated delayed fluorescence. Despite these substantial efforts, the research of their nonlinear optical properties and applications in ultrafast photonics devices is still in its infancy.

In this paper, we report the fabrication of mono-sized CNDs by carbonization of dipropylamine (DPA) in the nanochannels of zeolite ZnAlPO-43. The as-synthesized CNDs show excellent nonlinear saturable absorption property. The as-synthesized CNDs is an excellent SA for the generation of ultrafast laser for their advantages of simple fabrication, low cost, large modulation depth, and low saturation intensity. By applying CNDs SA into the erbium-doped fiber laser (EDFL) ring cavity, the ultra-stable mode-locked EDFL that operating on regular solitons or bound state solitons can be obtained. The excellent performance of fiber laser enables us to highlight the application of CNDs as an ideal SA in ultrafast phonic device.

The zinc aluminophosphate zeolite ZnAlPO-43 crystals were hydrothermally synthesized. Typically, zinc acetate dihydrate (99 %, Aldrich) and aluminum tri-isopropoxide (98 %) were mixed in distilled water under stirring for two hours. Then, phosphoric acid (85 wt%) and DPA (99 %) were added dropwisely into above solution. The molar ratio of the gel is 0.3 ZnO: 1.0 Al2O3: 1.0 P2O5: 2.1DPA: 300H2O. Then, the gel was transferred into Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for one day to yield ZnAlPO-43 crystals.

The ZnAlPO-43 crystals were transferred into a quartz crucible for carbonization. Then, we increased the temperature to 380 °C at a temperature ramp rate of 5 °C min−1 under a continuous argon flow. The sample was kept at 380 °C for 4 h and cooled to room temperature under an argon atmosphere. In our experiment, we found that the temperature 380 °C is the minimum synthesis temperature for the preparation of CNDs. In addition, the size of CNDs kept almost unchanged by further increasing the synthesis temperature due to the confinement of zeolite nanochannels. Therefore, the carbonization temperature about 380 °C was used to the fabrication of the CNDs, which is relative economic due to its low energy consumption. The CNDs@ZnAlPO-43 composite was obtained after carbonization.

The as-synthesized CNDs@ZnAlPO-43 sample was dissolved in 3 M NaOH under stirring for one hour. Then, the concentrated HCl was added under ultrasonic condition to release CNDs from template. After ultra-centrifugation and washing for eight times, the CNDs was prepared by freeze drying for 20 h.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) were carried out on a Bruker D8 system. Scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi FE-SEM Su-70) was used to analyze the morphology of ZnAlPO-43 crystal. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) were performed at 300 kV in a FEI TECNAI G2 60–300 equipment. Raman spectra was collected in a Horiba JY Labram HR800 using a 514.5 nm laser. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed in an ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer with Al Kα radiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental setup of EDFL and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The laser setup of EDFL based on CNDs SA is shown in Fig. 1 . A 976 nm laser diode (LD) was used as a pump source and coupled into the fiber laser cavity by using a 980/1550 nm wavelength division multiplexer (WDM). To ensure the best excitation state of EDF under the pump power of 500 mW, we selected 60 cm EDF (Coherent/Nufern EDFL-980-HP, with peak absorption of 25.0 dB/m @1530 nm, and doped concentration of erbium ion in fiber core of 4000 ppm) as the gain medium to build a ring fiber laser. The total cavity length was about 8.3 m. A polarization independent isolator (ISO) was insert into the cavity to make sure the unidirectional operation. The polarization state of light was adjusted by using a polarization controller (PC). A 10 % optical coupler (OC) was used to output the laser. The output lasers were analyzed by using a power meter, an optical spectrum analyzer (OSA, YOKOGAWAAQ6375B) with a resolution of 0.02 nm, a high-speed oscilloscope, an autocorrelator (Pulsecheck SM600) and a radio frequency (RF) spectrum analyzer (KEYSIGHT N9020B). The insert loss of the CNDs-SA was measured to be 1.4 dB. The optical damage threshold of this optical modulation device was calculated based on Q-switched erbium-doped fiber laser based on this CNDs SA, which is higher than 8 mJ/cm2.

Fig. 2 a shows the fabrication process of CNDs. Firstly, the ZnAlPO-43 crystals with high crystallinity were fabricated by using organic DPA agent. The experimental XRD pattern of ZnAlPO-43 matches well with the simulated XRD pattern and the previous reported XRD pattern of GIS[34,35] (Fig. 2b). The ZnAlPO-43 crystals have an octahedral morphology and their average size is about 100 μm (Fig. 2c). Then, the organic DPA that exists in the nanochannels of ZnAlPO-43 are transformed into CNDs after carbonization under 380 °C for 4 h. Finally, the isolated CNDs can be obtained by removing the ZnAlPO-43 template through chemical treatment.

Fig. 3 a shows the TEM image of the isolated CNDs, indicating their mono-sized (2 nm) dispersion with a lattice space of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of graphic[36]. The powder XRD pattern of CNDs shows a broad diffraction peak about 26° attributing to the (002) facet of graphite (Fig. 3b). As shown in Fig. 3c, there are two peaks at 1350 and 1590 cm−1 in the Raman spectrum of CNDs assigning to the D-band and G-band of graphene, respectively. The intensity ratio of the D-band to G-band of 0.9 implies a large portion of defects in CNDs[37]. There are two energy loss characteristics in the carbon K-edge EELS spectrum of CNDs (Fig. 3d). The existence of sp2-bonded carbon leads to the electronic transitions from carbon 1s to the π* (energy loss at around 285 eV) and to σ* molecular orbital (energy loss at around 295 eV), respectively.

The of isolated composition of CNDs was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS), as shown in Fig. 4 a. The XPS of CNDs shows three peaks at 284.8, 400 and 532 eV, assigning to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively[38]. The C 1s spectrum of CNDs can be deconvoluted into three peaks assigned to C–C/CC (284.7 eV), C-O/C-N (286.4 eV) and CO (289.1 eV), respectively (Fig. 4b). The high-resolution XPS N 1s spectrum consist of three components, including graphitic N (401.5 eV), pyrrolic N (400.2 eV) and pyridinic N (398.7 eV), respectively. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum can be assigned into three peaks, corresponding to C-O-C (533.7 eV), C-OH (532.3 eV) and CO (531.1 eV), as shown in Fig. 4d. These results illustrate that the CNDs contain N/O functional groups.

The nonlinear saturable absorption property of CNDs was investigated through a balanced twin detector measure ment using a 700 fs pulsed laser with 1.5 μm wavelength, as shown in Fig. 5 a. The obtained experimental data are fitted by the following formula[39]:
α
I
=
α s
/
1 + I /
I
sat
+
α n
where, α(I) is the nonlinear transmission, αs is the modulation depth (total change in nonlinear absorption), I is the input laser intensity, Isat is the saturation power intensity (defined as the optical intensity required in a steady state to reduce the absorption to half of its unbleached value), αn is the nonsaturable loss. Based on the fitting results, the modulation depth, saturable intensity, and nonsaturable loss of CNDs are determined to be 33 % 3.1 MW/cm2, and 7 %, respectively (Fig. 5b). The relatively large modulation depth maybe due to the uniform size distribution of CNDs. The large modulation depth and high ratio of saturable-to-nonsaturable absorption indicate stronger noise suppression and pulse shaping ability of SA, which are favor for the mode-locking laser generation. The relatively low saturable intensity of SA makes the decreasing of threshold of pulsed fiber laser. The relatively low nonsaturable loss is favor for the increasing of output power of laser. The above results indicate that CNDs has excellent nonlinear saturable absorption property and can be used as an ideal SA for ultrafast fiber laser generation.

By appropriately adjusting the PC, the stable mode-locked EDFL can be obtained at the pump power of 24 mW. The characteristics of output pulse at the pump power of 80 mW are shown in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6a shows the oscilloscope traces of the mode-locked pulse trains with a fixed time interval of 42 ns. The corresponding repetition rate is 23.8 MHz, which matches well with the laser cavity (8.3 m). The operating central wavelength is about 1557.8 nm with a 3 dB bandwidth of 12.47 nm (Fig. 6b). The distinct Kelley sidebands in output spectrum indicates the mode-locked fiber laser is operating in the soliton regime. The autocorrelation trace after fitting by a sech2 function is shown in Fig. 6c, which demonstrates that the pulse duration is 388 fs. The SNR of the RF spectrum is over 84.4 dB and higher than that in other researches using low-dimensional as the SA[40–43], indicating the excellent stability of the mode-locked EDFL (Fig. 6d).

Fig. 7 (a) shows the dynamic evolution of the output spectrum within 5 days to check the stability of the mode-locked EDFL during long-term operation. Neither the drift of the central wavelength nor the variation of the 3 dB spectral width indicates the high stability of the laser. Meanwhile, the measured output power is almost linearly related to the pump power, as shown in Fig. 7b. By increasing the pump power from 24 to 180 mW, the output power increases almost linearly from 0.28 to 9.37 mW, resulting in a slope efficiency of 6 %.

By increasing the pump power to 97 mW, the transition from regular solitons to bound state solitons can be observed by adjusting the polarization state of PC carefully. The formation process is that the two adjacent solitons interact with each other directly to form bounded-state pulses, and then the bound-state pulses arranged themselves regularly in the cavity [41]. Fig. 8 a shows the oscilloscope trace of the bound state pulse trains with a fixed time interval of 42 ns, which corresponds to a repetition rate of 23.8 MHz. The output optical spectrum exhibits the modulation period is 6.54 nm at the central wavelength of 1558.2 nm (Fig. 8b). The autocorrelation trace shows that there are three peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:2:1 (Fig. 8c). The single pulse duration is measured to be 422 fs after fitting by a sech2 function and the pulse separation of the solitons is 1.23 ps. Meanwhile, according to Fourier transform theory, the pulse separation(ΔT) and the modulation period (Δλ) are related by the following formula[44]:
Δ T = λ
c 2
/
( C · Δ λ )
Here, λc is the central wavelength and C is the speed of light. The calculated theoretical pulse separation is about 1.24 ps, which is basically consistent with the value depicted in Fig. 8c. Fig. 8d shows the RF spectrum of the bound state solitons. The SNR reaches to 84.1 dB at the repetition rate of 23.8 MHz, which indicates the highly stable bound state solitons of the EDFL based on CNDs SA.

Finally, we summarized the performance of mode-locked EDFLs based on different SA types. From Table 1 , it can be seen that the CNDs laser has a narrow pulse duration and high SNR, which can be of favor for generating the ultra-stable femtosecond pulses. We have also compared the saturable absorber data of some other good articles, and our material is comparable to them[45–47]. In addition, the threshold power of 24 mW is relatively low compared to the other reported works, which demonstrates the strong nonlinear optical properties of CNDs. In conclusion, all these clearly show that CNDs is an ideal candidate for SA in fiber laser.

In summary, the CNDs were successfully synthesized from a dipropylamine-templated zeolite precursor calcination under 380 °C. The as-synthesized mono-sized CNDs have a large modulation depth, low saturable intensity, and low nonsaturable loss, which make them an excellent SA nanomaterial for the application in ultrafast fiber laser generation. By inserting the CNDs SA into the EDFL ring cavity, the mode-locked EDFL with a pulse duration of 388 fs, a central wavelength of 1557.8 nm, a 3 dB spectral width of 12.47 nm and a SNR of 84.4 dB was experimentally obtained. Moreover, stable soliton pairs with single-pulse width of 422 fs and SNR of 84.1 dB can also be achieved by using the CNDs as SA. This work reveals that the CNDs contain the efficacy of being used as an ideal SA and have promising potential for use in ultrafast photonics.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61705134), Shenzhen Science and Technology Program (No. RCBS20221008093109034) and Pingshan District Innovation Platform Project of Shenzhen Hi-tech Zone Development Special Plan in 2022 (No. 29853M-KCJ-2023-002-01).
